source_text: Marco, speak more slowly, please!

reference_translations.text: Marco, parla più lentamente, per favore!
reference_translations.notes: Standard informal tu command; -are verb takes -a.

reference_translations.text: Marco, parli più lentamente, per favore!
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: parli is the present tu / formal Lei, not the informal command parla.

explanation: Addressing Marco by name signals the informal tu. The informal tu command of an -are verb ends in -a: parlare gives parla. The present-tense tu parli would be the wrong choice here.

source_text: Anna, open the window, it's hot in here!

reference_translations.text: Anna, apri la finestra, fa caldo qui!
reference_translations.notes: Informal tu; -ire verb takes -i (apri).

reference_translations.text: Anna, apri la finestra, fa caldo qui dentro!
reference_translations.notes: Variant with 'qui dentro' for 'in here'.

explanation: Anna's name marks the informal tu. The informal tu command of an -ire verb ends in -i: aprire gives apri. Note this is the opposite vowel from the -are verbs (parla).

source_text: It's late, let's go home now!

reference_translations.text: È tardi, andiamo a casa adesso!
reference_translations.notes: noi command = present noi (andiamo), 'let's go'.

reference_translations.text: È tardi, torniamo a casa adesso!
reference_translations.notes: Variant: torniamo ('let's go back/return') is equally natural.

explanation: A 'let's...' invitation uses the noi command, which equals the present-tense noi. Andare gives andiamo, 'let's go'. No special ending is needed for the noi command.

source_text: Children, wait here, I'll be right back!

reference_translations.text: Bambini, aspettate qui, torno subito!
reference_translations.notes: voi command = present voi (aspettate).

reference_translations.text: Ragazzi, aspettate qui, torno subito!
reference_translations.notes: Variant: ragazzi for a group of children/kids.

explanation: Addressing a group ('children') calls for the voi command, which equals the present-tense voi: aspettare gives aspettate. Like the noi command, the voi form reuses the ordinary present.

source_text: Luca, finish your homework before dinner!

reference_translations.text: Luca, finisci i compiti prima di cena!
reference_translations.notes: Informal tu; finire is an -isc- verb (finisci).

reference_translations.text: Luca, finisci i compiti prima di cena!
reference_translations.notes: Same; 'i compiti' renders 'your homework' naturally.

explanation: Luca's name marks the informal tu. Finire is an -isc- verb, so its informal tu command inserts -isc- before the ending: finisci. The plain -i of the -ire verbs still applies, with -isc- in front.

source_text: Giulia, don't speak with your mouth full!

reference_translations.text: Giulia, non parlare con la bocca piena!
reference_translations.notes: Negative tu = non + infinitive (non parlare).

reference_translations.text: Giulia, non parla con la bocca piena!
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: negates the positive command; the negative tu uses the infinitive, non parlare.

explanation: Telling Giulia (informal tu) NOT to do something uses non plus the infinitive: non parlare. The infinitive parlare, not the positive command parla, drives the negative tu form.

source_text: Don't open the door to strangers!

reference_translations.text: Non aprire la porta agli sconosciuti!
reference_translations.notes: Negative tu = non + infinitive (non aprire).

reference_translations.text: Non aprire la porta agli estranei!
reference_translations.notes: Variant: estranei is a synonym for sconosciuti.

explanation: A negative tu command is non plus the infinitive: non aprire, 'don't open'. The infinitive aprire carries the command; non apri (negating the present) would be the typical mistake.

source_text: Don't make noise, the baby is sleeping!

reference_translations.text: Non fare rumore, il bambino dorme!
reference_translations.notes: Negative tu of fare = non + infinitive (non fare), even though the positive is the irregular fa'.

reference_translations.text: Non fare rumore, il bimbo dorme!
reference_translations.notes: Variant: bimbo is a common diminutive for bambino.

explanation: Even verbs with an irregular positive command obey the negative rule. Fare's positive tu is the short fa', but the negative tu is the plain infinitive: non fare. The irregular short form disappears under negation.

source_text: Kids, don't forget the keys!

reference_translations.text: Ragazzi, non dimenticate le chiavi!
reference_translations.notes: Negative voi = non + affirmative voi (non dimenticate).

reference_translations.text: Ragazzi, non dimenticatevi le chiavi!
reference_translations.notes: Variant with the reflexive -vi attached; clitic placement is scored in the pronoun tree, not here.

explanation: Addressing a group ('kids', informal voi), the negative command is simply non plus the affirmative: dimenticare gives the positive voi dimenticate, so 'don't forget' is non dimenticate. Only the tu form uses non + infinitive; voi negates its normal command.

source_text: Pietro, go home, it's already late!

reference_translations.text: Pietro, va' a casa, è già tardi!
reference_translations.notes: Irregular short tu of andare: va' (apostrophed).

reference_translations.text: Pietro, vai a casa, è già tardi!
reference_translations.notes: The full form vai is equally accepted.

explanation: Pietro's name marks the informal tu. Andare has a special short command: va' (with an apostrophe), and the full vai is also fine. Both mean 'go!'.

source_text: Marta, tell the truth, it's important!

reference_translations.text: Marta, di' la verità, è importante!
reference_translations.notes: Irregular short tu of dire: di' (the only form; no long alternative).

reference_translations.text: Marta, di' sempre la verità, è importante!
reference_translations.notes: Variant adding sempre ('always') for emphasis.

explanation: Marta's name marks the informal tu. Dire has the shortest command, di' ('say/tell'), and unlike va'/vai it has no long alternative. The present-tense dici is not a command.

source_text: Sara, be patient, the bus is about to arrive!

reference_translations.text: Sara, abbi pazienza, l'autobus sta per arrivare!
reference_translations.notes: Irregular tu of avere: abbi (abbi pazienza).

reference_translations.text: Sara, abbi un po' di pazienza, l'autobus sta per arrivare!
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'un po' di pazienza'.

explanation: Sara's name marks the informal tu. Avere has the irregular command abbi, as in abbi pazienza ('be patient'). It is a form to learn rather than a clipped one; the present-tense hai is different.

source_text: Tommaso, be good at school today!

reference_translations.text: Tommaso, sii bravo a scuola oggi!
reference_translations.notes: Irregular tu of essere: sii (sii bravo).

reference_translations.text: Tommaso, fa' il bravo a scuola oggi!
reference_translations.notes: Idiomatic variant 'fare il bravo'; uses fa' instead, also a valid command but tests the fare short form rather than sii.

explanation: Tommaso's name marks the informal tu. Essere has the irregular command sii, 'be!', as in sii bravo ('be good'). Don't confuse it with the present-tense sei.

source_text: Please speak more slowly, sir.

reference_translations.text: Parli più lentamente, per favore, signore.
reference_translations.notes: Formal Lei command = present subjunctive (parli).

reference_translations.text: Per favore, parli più lentamente, signore.
reference_translations.notes: Word-order variant; parli unchanged.

explanation: Addressing 'sir' (signore) signals the formal Lei. The Lei command uses the present subjunctive: parlare gives parli. It looks like the present-tense tu but here it is the respectful command; the informal version would be parla.

source_text: Please come in, madam, and make yourself comfortable.

reference_translations.text: Prego, venga, signora, e si accomodi.
reference_translations.notes: Formal Lei command venga (subjunctive of venire); si accomodi is the formal reflexive command.

reference_translations.text: Venga pure, signora, si accomodi.
reference_translations.notes: Variant with 'pure' softening the invitation.

explanation: Addressing 'madam' (signora) signals the formal Lei. The Lei command borrows the present subjunctive, and for the irregular venire that is venga, 'come in'. It is built from the subjunctive stem, not from the informal vieni.

source_text: Go straight on until the traffic light, sir.

reference_translations.text: Vada sempre dritto fino al semaforo, signore.
reference_translations.notes: Formal Lei directions: vada (subjunctive of andare).

reference_translations.text: Prosegua dritto fino al semaforo, signore.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: proseguire (prosegua) is common in directions.

explanation: Giving directions to 'sir' (signore) calls for the formal Lei. The Lei command uses the present subjunctive: andare gives vada, 'go'. The informal short va' would be wrong for this respectful register.

source_text: Come on, you can do it!

reference_translations.text: Dai, ce la puoi fare!
reference_translations.notes: dai = the frozen encouragement marker 'come on'.

reference_translations.text: Forza, ce la puoi fare!
reference_translations.notes: forza is an equally natural encouragement marker.

explanation: 'Dai!' (or 'Forza!') is the frozen marker for 'come on', pure encouragement with no literal 'give' left in it.

source_text: Excuse me, do you know where the station is?

reference_translations.text: Senta, scusi, sa dov'è la stazione?
reference_translations.notes: Senta = the formal attention-getter to a stranger (Lei).

reference_translations.text: Mi scusi, sa dov'è la stazione?
reference_translations.notes: Also natural; opens with the apology instead of senta.

explanation: 'Senta, scusi...' opens a request to a stranger: senta is the formal frozen 'listen' that gets attention politely.

source_text: Go right ahead and take a seat.

reference_translations.text: Prenda pure una sedia.
reference_translations.notes: Formal (Lei) prenda + the softener pure ('go right ahead').

reference_translations.text: Si accomodi pure.
reference_translations.notes: Idiomatic; si accomodi is the set formal phrase, with pure softening.

explanation: 'Prenda pure' pairs the formal command with pure, the particle that turns an order into warm permission.

source_text: Look, I swear it's true.

reference_translations.text: Guarda, te lo giuro, è vero.
reference_translations.notes: guarda = the frozen emphasis marker 'look'.

reference_translations.text: Senti, te lo giuro, è vero.
reference_translations.notes: senti works the same way as an emphasis/attention marker.

explanation: 'Guarda...' here is not 'look' literally; it is the frozen marker that presses a point home.

source_text: Come on kids, let's go, it's late!

reference_translations.text: Su ragazzi, andiamo, è tardi!
reference_translations.notes: su = the chivvying marker; andiamo is the noi command.

reference_translations.text: Dai ragazzi, andiamo, è tardi!
reference_translations.notes: dai is equally natural as the nudge.

explanation: 'Su!' (or 'Dai!') chivvies people along; here it frames the noi command andiamo ('let's go').

source_text: Please, do tell me everything.

reference_translations.text: Mi dica pure tutto.
reference_translations.notes: Formal dica + pure inviting them to go ahead.

reference_translations.text: Mi dica tutto, prego.
reference_translations.notes: Also natural, with prego carrying the politeness.

explanation: 'Mi dica pure' is the gentle formal prompt: dica (Lei) with pure inviting them to speak freely.

source_text: Mix the ingredients well.

reference_translations.text: Mescolare bene gli ingredienti.
reference_translations.notes: Recipe register: bare infinitive mescolare.

reference_translations.text: Mescola bene gli ingredienti.
reference_translations.notes: Only if addressing one person directly (tu); the recipe norm is the infinitive.

explanation: A recipe step is an impersonal instruction, so Italian uses the bare infinitive: Mescolare, not a personal command.

source_text: Store in a cool, dry place.

reference_translations.text: Conservare in luogo fresco e asciutto.
reference_translations.notes: Label instruction: bare infinitive conservare.

explanation: Product labels address no one in particular, so the instruction is the infinitive conservare.

source_text: No smoking.

reference_translations.text: Non fumare.
reference_translations.notes: Public prohibition: non + infinitive.

reference_translations.text: Vietato fumare.
reference_translations.notes: Equally standard sign wording.

explanation: A public sign uses non + the bare infinitive: Non fumare, the impersonal prohibition form.

source_text: Do not touch the works of art.

reference_translations.text: Non toccare le opere d'arte.
reference_translations.notes: Museum sign: non + infinitive.

explanation: Like 'Non fumare', a museum prohibition uses non + the infinitive, addressing every visitor impersonally.

source_text: Madam, please speak more slowly.

reference_translations.text: Signora, parli più lentamente, per favore.
reference_translations.notes: 'Signora' -> formal, so the Lei form parli.

reference_translations.text: Signora, parla più lentamente.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG register: parla is the informal tu, but Signora takes Lei.

explanation: 'Signora' is a formal address, so the imperative is the Lei form parli, not the informal parla.

source_text: Marco, speak more slowly!

reference_translations.text: Marco, parla più lentamente!
reference_translations.notes: A first name -> informal tu, so parla.

reference_translations.text: Marco, parli più lentamente!
reference_translations.notes: WRONG register: parli is the formal Lei; a first name takes tu.

explanation: Addressing Marco by name is informal (tu), so the command is parla, not the formal parli. The same English sentence flips form with the addressee.

source_text: Excuse me, go straight on until the traffic light.

reference_translations.text: Mi scusi, vada sempre dritto fino al semaforo.
reference_translations.notes: To a stranger (Lei): vada.

reference_translations.text: Scusa, va' sempre dritto fino al semaforo.
reference_translations.notes: Informal variant if addressing a friend; the polite version uses vada.

explanation: Stopping a stranger is formal, so andare takes the Lei form vada, not the informal va'.

source_text: Anna, be patient!

reference_translations.text: Anna, abbi pazienza!
reference_translations.notes: First name -> tu, so abbi.

reference_translations.text: Anna, abbia pazienza!
reference_translations.notes: WRONG register: abbia is the formal Lei.

explanation: A first name is informal, so avere takes abbi, not the formal abbia.

source_text: Doctor, please be kind.

reference_translations.text: Dottore, sia gentile, per favore.
reference_translations.notes: 'Dottore' -> formal, so sia.

reference_translations.text: Dottore, sii gentile.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG register: sii is the informal tu.

explanation: 'Dottore' is a formal address, so essere takes the Lei form sia, not the informal sii.

source_text: Grandpa, wait for me here!

reference_translations.text: Nonno, aspettami qui!
reference_translations.notes: Family -> informal tu; aspetta (with the clitic mi).

reference_translations.text: Nonno, aspetta qui!
reference_translations.notes: Also fine without the clitic; the point is the informal tu form.

explanation: A grandparent is family, addressed with tu, so aspettare takes aspetta, not the formal aspetti.
